posts.title: How Can I Design My Book? (What Nobody Tells First-Time Authors)
posts.metaDescription: Finished writing your book and wondering what comes next? Here's an honest, no-fluff guide to what book design actually involves — and how to get it right.
posts.contentHtml:
You spent months — maybe years — writing your book. You finally typed the last sentence, closed your laptop, and felt that rare mix of exhaustion and relief. Then someone asked, "So what does your book look like?"

And you had absolutely nothing to say.

This is where most first-time authors get stuck. Writing the book is the part everyone talks about. Designing it? That's the part people figure out last-minute, usually while panicking.

So let's slow down and actually talk through it.

Design Is Not Just the Cover

When people say "book design," they almost always picture the cover. And the cover is important — we'll get to that. But the cover is only half the job.

The other half is your interior layout. That's everything inside your book: the fonts, the spacing, the margins, how your chapters open, where your page numbers sit, what your copyright page looks like. All of it is design. All of it affects how a reader experiences your book.

A book with a stunning cover and a messy interior is like a restaurant with beautiful décor and terrible food. The outside got you in, but you're not coming back. Readers feel the same way. They might not be able to articulate why your formatting feels off, but they'll feel it — and it creates doubt about the quality of your writing, even if the writing is excellent.

What the Interior Layout Actually Involves

Most authors assume they can format their book in Microsoft Word and be done with it. Sometimes that works. Usually it doesn't — at least not to a professional standard.

Interior book design involves choosing the right typeface for your genre (a serif font like Garamond reads very differently from a clean sans-serif), setting margins that account for how the book will be bound, making sure your chapter openers are consistent and intentional, and handling the front and back matter correctly — that's your title page, copyright page, table of contents, dedication, acknowledgments, about the author section, and so on.

There's also the technical side: your file needs to be exported correctly for whatever platform you're printing or distributing through. Amazon KDP has specific requirements. IngramSpark has different ones. Get the settings wrong and your book comes back looking nothing like what you designed.

The Cover Is Your First (and Sometimes Only) Impression

On Amazon, your cover is displayed as a thumbnail roughly the size of a postage stamp. That's the version most people will see before they decide whether to click.

A good cover communicates genre, mood, and professionalism — all within a couple of seconds. It doesn't need to be flashy. It needs to be right. A thriller cover looks nothing like a romance cover, which looks nothing like a business book. Each genre has visual language that readers recognize subconsciously. Break those conventions without understanding them and your book gets ignored.

The cover also needs to work at full size — on your website, in promotional materials, in physical bookstores if you're going that route. And for print, you need a full cover spread: front, spine, and back, all sized exactly to your page count and trim dimensions.

So Can You Do It Yourself?

Honestly, it depends on what you're trying to accomplish.

If you're putting together an eBook for a small audience and you just want something clean and readable, you can absolutely DIY it. Canva and Vellum are popular options that don't require any design expertise, and they produce decent results for straightforward projects.

But if you're printing physical copies, publishing on multiple platforms, or building any kind of author brand around this book — DIY starts showing its limits quickly. The files won't always meet print specs. The typography choices won't always be right for your genre. And the hours you spend fighting with formatting software are hours you're not spending on your next book, your marketing, or anything else that actually moves your career forward.

Where Literary Lab Comes In

This is exactly what we do at Literary Lab. We handle book formatting and cover design for independent authors who want their book to look like it came from a traditional publisher — not because they used a fancy template, but because the design was built specifically for their book.

We work across genres: fiction, non-fiction, memoir, business, children's books. Every project is handled in Adobe InDesign, which gives us the level of control that actually matters in print production. And we deliver print-ready files that meet the specs for KDP, IngramSpark, and wherever else you're publishing.

If you're at the point where you know you want this done properly, get in touch with us here. We'll walk you through the process and give you a clear picture of what we can do for your specific book.

Designing a book isn't something most authors are trained to do — and there's no shame in that. Writing is your craft. Design is ours. The best books happen when both are done well.

Literary Lab is a book design and formatting studio for independent authors.

posts.title: What Makes a Good Book Design? Here's What Actually Matters
posts.metaDescription: Good book design is harder to define than you'd think — but you always know it when you see it. Here's what separates a polished, professional book from one that screams "self-published."
posts.contentHtml:
There's a version of this question that sounds simple. What makes a good book design? Just make it look nice, right?

Not quite.

Good book design is actually about making things invisible. When a book is designed well, you don't notice the fonts, the spacing, or the margins. You just... read. You're pulled into the story or the ideas, and the design quietly does its job in the background. The moment design calls attention to itself — something feels off about the spacing, the font looks wrong, the cover doesn't match the genre — the spell breaks.

So what actually goes into getting it right? Here's what matters most.

Typography Sets the Entire Tone

Most people don't think about fonts when they read a book, but they feel them. The typeface you choose for your body text affects how long someone can read comfortably, how serious or playful your book feels, and whether the whole thing looks like a professional publication or a Word document someone printed at home.

For most books, a classic serif font is the right call. Garamond, Caslon, Minion Pro — these have been used in print for centuries because they work. They guide the eye across a line of text in a way that's effortless for the reader. The size, the line spacing, the spacing between letters — all of it gets calibrated for the specific trim size and genre of your book. A novel formatted at 5×8 needs different settings than a business book formatted at 6×9.

Get typography wrong and everything else suffers. Get it right and no one will ever comment on it — which is exactly the goal.

Margins and Spacing Are More Important Than They Look

One of the most common signs of amateur book formatting is margins that are too narrow. The author or designer squeezed the text to save pages, and the result is a book that feels suffocating to read.

Margins serve a real purpose. The inner margin — the one closest to the spine — needs to be wide enough that text doesn't disappear into the binding. The outer margin gives readers a place to hold the book without their thumbs covering words. The top and bottom margins frame the text block on the page so it feels balanced and intentional.

White space in general isn't wasted space. It's breathing room. It's what makes a page feel readable rather than crammed. Professional book designers think carefully about where that space goes and why — and the difference shows every time you open the book.

The Cover Needs to Do a Specific Job

A lot of authors think a "good" cover just means something that looks attractive. But attractive isn't the same as effective. A cover that works is one that does a very specific job: it signals the genre, sets the mood, and makes someone want to know more — all within about two seconds.

Every genre has its own visual language. Thrillers use dark tones, sharp typography, and a sense of tension. Romance covers are warm, often feature people, and use softer color palettes. Business books tend to go clean and bold. If your cover doesn't speak that language, readers in your genre will scroll right past it.

And it all needs to hold up as a thumbnail. On Amazon, your cover is tiny. If the title isn't legible at that size, or the image turns into an indistinct blur, you've already lost the click. Good cover design accounts for every size and context it'll appear in.

Consistency Across Every Page

This one sounds obvious but gets missed constantly. Good book design is consistent from the first page to the last. Chapter openers all look the same. Heading styles don't drift. Section breaks use the same ornament or spacing throughout. Page numbers sit in the same spot on every page.

Inconsistency is the design equivalent of typos. Readers don't always catch it consciously, but it accumulates as a feeling that something is slightly off. That feeling chips away at trust in the book — and by extension, the author.

A professionally designed book holds its visual logic from cover to cover. Every decision that was made on page one is honored on page two hundred.

The Technical Stuff That Has to Be Right

Even a beautifully designed book fails if the files aren't set up correctly for printing. This is the part nobody talks about until something goes wrong.

Print-ready files need to be exported as PDF/X with fonts embedded, images at 300 DPI, and correct bleed settings for the cover. The trim size needs to match exactly what your printer or distributor requires. KDP and IngramSpark both have specific technical requirements — and if your files don't meet them, your book either gets rejected or comes back with quality issues.

This is one of the biggest reasons authors hire professionals. Not because they can't learn it, but because the stakes are real and the margin for error is small.

What We Do at Literary Lab

At Literary Lab, we've built our entire process around getting all of this right — not just the parts that are visible, but the technical foundation underneath.

We work with authors on interior formatting and cover design, and we treat every project as its own thing. Your book isn't going through a template machine. It's getting a layout built for its specific genre, length, and publishing goals.

If you want to see what a properly designed book looks and feels like — or you're ready to get yours done — reach out to us here.

Good book design doesn't shout. It just works. And when it works well, the only thing readers notice is how much they're enjoying your book.

Literary Lab is a book design and formatting studio for independent authors.

posts.title: What Software Can I Use to Create a Book? An Honest Breakdown
posts.metaDescription: From Word to InDesign, there's no shortage of tools for creating a book. But which one is actually right for you? Here's a straight answer with no fluff.
posts.contentHtml:
This is one of the first questions authors Google when they realize writing the book was only half the job. There are more options than you'd expect — and most of them come with trade-offs nobody mentions upfront.

Here's an honest look at the tools people actually use to create books, who they're best for, and where each one falls short.

Microsoft Word — The Default That Most People Outgrow

Nearly every author starts here because they already have it. Word is where the manuscript lives, so it feels natural to just keep going and format it there too.

And for simple eBooks or internal documents, it's fine. Word can produce a clean enough result if you know what you're doing with styles, section breaks, and page setup. The problem is that "knowing what you're doing" in Word for book formatting is a whole skill in itself — one that most people underestimate. Things like mirror margins, chapter-specific headers, proper widow and orphan control, and PDF export settings are not intuitive, and getting them wrong produces files that printers and distributors reject.

Word is a word processor, not a layout tool. It was built for documents, not books. That distinction matters more the more serious your publishing goals are.

Canva — Great for Social Media, Limited for Books

Canva has gotten genuinely impressive over the last few years, and it's a popular choice for authors who want a visual drag-and-drop experience without a learning curve. For simple projects — a short eBook, a workbook, a lead magnet — it can produce something decent.

But Canva has real limitations when it comes to full book production. It doesn't handle long-form text well. There's no proper baseline grid, no paragraph style system, and no reliable way to manage a 300-page manuscript without things getting messy fast. The export options are improving but still don't consistently meet the PDF/X standards required for commercial printing.

If you're using Canva to mock up your cover or create marketing materials for your book, great. If you're trying to format your entire novel in it, you're going to hit walls.

Vellum — The Favorite for Fiction Authors on Mac

Vellum is the tool a lot of indie fiction authors swear by, and for good reason. It's clean, intuitive, and specifically built for book formatting. You import your manuscript, choose a style, and it handles most of the heavy lifting. The output looks genuinely professional for eBook formats — Kindle, EPUB, Apple Books — and it does a solid job with print as well.

The catch is that it's Mac-only and not free. There's a one-time purchase price that's reasonable if you're formatting multiple books, less so if you're doing just one. And while Vellum is excellent for straightforward fiction formatting, it has less flexibility for books that need complex layouts — heavy non-fiction, illustrated books, anything that doesn't follow a standard novel structure.

For a self-publishing novelist on a Mac who wants clean, fast results without hiring a designer, Vellum is probably the best DIY option available.

Adobe InDesign — The Industry Standard

This is what professional book designers use. InDesign is built specifically for layout and publication design, and it shows — the level of control it gives you over typography, spacing, master pages, and print settings is simply not available in any other tool.

The trade-off is that InDesign has a steep learning curve. It's not software you pick up in an afternoon. Understanding how to use paragraph styles, set up master pages, manage a long document correctly, and export print-ready files takes real time and practice. For most authors, learning InDesign from scratch just to format one book isn't worth it.

But for professional designers — and for anyone who wants results that genuinely match traditionally published books — InDesign is the tool. It's what we use at Literary Lab for every project we take on.

Atticus — The New All-in-One Option

Atticus is a newer tool that's been gaining traction in the indie publishing community. It works on any platform (unlike Vellum), handles both eBook and print formatting, and is designed to be simple enough for non-designers. It's priced as a one-time purchase and has been improving steadily.

It won't give you the same output quality as InDesign, but it's a solid middle-ground option for authors who want to format their own books without hiring someone and without learning professional design software. If you're a self-publisher on a budget who wants decent results with a reasonable time investment, Atticus is worth looking at.

So Which One Should You Use?

It depends on what you're trying to achieve. If you're formatting a short eBook with no complex layout needs, Canva or even Word might get you there. If you're a Mac user writing genre fiction and you want clean, professional results without hiring a designer, Vellum is the answer. If you want the highest quality output possible — especially for print — InDesign is the standard, which means hiring someone who knows it.

That's where we come in. At Literary Lab, we use Adobe InDesign for every book we format because it's the only tool that gives us the precision and control that print production demands. Whether you're publishing a novel, a memoir, or a business book, the result is a file that looks professionally made — because it is.

If you'd rather skip the software learning curve entirely and just get your book done right, let's talk.

The right software is the one that matches your skill level, your timeline, and your publishing goals. Just don't let the tool be the reason your book doesn't look as good as it deserves to.

Literary Lab is a book design and formatting studio for independent authors.

posts.title: What Are the Popular Book Design Trends Right Now?
posts.metaDescription: Book design trends shift more than most authors realize. Here's what's working in publishing right now — and what readers are responding to in 2025.
posts.contentHtml:
Publishing has always had its visual moments. Certain eras produced certain looks — the pulpy paperback covers of the 60s, the embossed gold-lettered spines of the 80s, the photographic realism that dominated early 2000s fiction covers. Design in books moves slower than in, say, social media — but it does move.

Right now, there are some clear trends shaping what readers expect when they pick up a book, and what makes a cover stop someone mid-scroll on Amazon. If you're designing a book in 2025, these are the directions worth paying attention to.

Bold, Typographic Covers Are Everywhere

For a while, covers relied heavily on imagery — a lone figure in a landscape, a close-up photograph, a dramatic scene. That's still around, but there's been a strong shift toward covers where the typography itself is the design.

You've probably seen it: big, confident lettering that takes up most of the cover, sometimes overlapping, sometimes playing with scale, sometimes using only two or three colors. It's a look that works especially well for literary fiction, essays, memoirs, and non-fiction. It feels editorial — like something you'd see in a magazine spread — and it reads clearly at thumbnail size because there's nothing competing with the text.

This trend is partly a response to how books are discovered now. On a phone screen, intricate illustrated covers can turn into visual noise. A bold typographic cover cuts through it.

Illustration Is Making a Serious Comeback

On the other end of the spectrum, hand-drawn and illustrated covers have come back in a big way — particularly in literary fiction, young adult, and fantasy. There's a warmth and distinctiveness to illustration that stock photography simply can't replicate, and readers have started noticing.

The style varies widely. Some illustrated covers go detailed and painterly, almost like fine art. Others use flat, graphic shapes with a more modern sensibility. What they share is a sense of craft — a feeling that someone made a deliberate creative choice for this specific book, rather than picking the best image from a licensing library.

For indie authors, this trend is actually good news. Commissioning an illustrator for a cover used to feel out of reach budget-wise. The market has opened up significantly, and a distinctive illustrated cover can make a self-published book stand out in ways that generic stock-photo covers never could.

Minimalism in Book Interiors Is the New Standard

Inside the book, the trend has moved firmly toward clean and uncluttered. Readers have less patience than ever for dense, airless pages — and the success of clean, well-spaced interiors in literary and non-fiction publishing has set a new standard that readers now expect.

That means generous margins, comfortable line spacing, and restrained use of decorative elements. Chapter openers are simpler than they were a decade ago — a clean chapter number, maybe a subtle ornament, nothing that feels overdone. The focus is entirely on readability. If a design element isn't making the text easier to engage with, it gets cut.

This isn't boring — it's disciplined. The best interiors feel effortless to read, and that effortlessness is the result of a lot of careful decisions working quietly in the background.

Color Has Gotten Bolder and More Unexpected

Pastel covers dominated indie publishing for a stretch — soft pinks, dusty blues, muted greens. That era isn't completely over, but there's been a clear push toward more saturated, unexpected color choices. Deep terracotta. Acid yellow. Burnt orange paired with black. Colors that feel confident rather than safe.

This shift is showing up in both fiction and non-fiction, and it's being driven by the same logic as the typographic trend — standing out in a grid of thumbnails. When every other book on the page is soft and muted, a cover with a strong, unusual color choice commands attention without even trying.

The key is that the color still has to make sense for the book. Bold for the sake of bold doesn't work. But when the color choice connects to the subject or tone of the book, it can be incredibly effective.

Texture and Tactile Finishes for Print Editions

This one is specific to physical books, but it's worth mentioning because it's changing how readers experience the object itself. Spot UV coating, soft-touch matte lamination, foil stamping, embossing — publishers and designers are investing more in how a book feels in your hands, not just how it looks.

For indie authors printing through services like IngramSpark, some of these finishes are accessible. A soft-touch matte cover, for example, makes a self-published book feel premium in a way that a standard gloss finish doesn't. It's a small detail that readers notice immediately when they pick it up.

Staying Current Without Chasing Trends

One thing worth saying: trends are useful to know, but chasing them blindly can make your book feel generic in a different way. The best-designed books take what's working in the market and filter it through the specific identity of the book itself. Genre, tone, audience — all of that shapes which trends apply and which ones don't.

At Literary Lab, we pay close attention to what's happening in book design because it directly affects how we approach each project. We're not here to put your book in a template — we're here to design something that fits your book and connects with your readers.

If you want a cover or interior that feels current without feeling derivative, get in touch with us. We'd love to talk through what your book needs.

Trends come and go, but good design is always about the same thing: making your book impossible to ignore and easy to love.

Literary Lab is a book design and formatting studio for independent authors.

posts.title: Can You Make a Book on Canva? The Honest Answer
posts.metaDescription: Canva makes book design look easy — but is it actually good enough for publishing? Here's what Canva can and can't do when it comes to creating a real book.
posts.contentHtml:
Canva has become the go-to design tool for people who aren't designers. It's free, it's drag-and-drop, and it makes things look decent without any real learning curve. So when authors ask whether they can just use Canva to make their book, the question makes complete sense.

The honest answer is: it depends on what kind of book you're making and what you're trying to do with it.

What Canva Is Actually Good At

Let's start with what works. Canva is genuinely solid for certain parts of the book creation process.

For covers, it's a reasonable starting point. The template library is large, the design tools are intuitive, and if you have a good eye, you can put together something that looks presentable. A lot of authors use Canva to mock up cover concepts before passing them to a professional designer — and that's a completely sensible workflow.

For eBooks that are more visual than text-heavy — think workbooks, planners, short guides, lead magnets — Canva does a decent job. You have control over layout, you can mix text and images freely, and the PDF export is usually clean enough for digital distribution.

If your book is short, simple, and primarily going to live as a digital file, Canva can get you to a usable result.

Where Canva Starts to Fall Apart

The problems show up when you try to use Canva for something it wasn't built for: a full-length book going to print.

Long-form text management in Canva is genuinely painful. There's no real paragraph style system, which means if you want to change your body font across a 200-page document, you're doing it manually. There's no baseline grid to keep your text aligned consistently from page to page. There's no proper widow and orphan control, which means you'll regularly end up with single lines floating awkwardly at the top or bottom of a page.

The export situation is also a real issue for print. Commercial printers and platforms like IngramSpark require PDF/X files with specific bleed settings, embedded fonts, and CMYK color profiles. Canva's export doesn't reliably meet these requirements. The cover trim and bleed dimensions need to match your exact page count and paper weight — Canva doesn't dynamically calculate this for you. Authors who try to submit Canva files for print often find their files rejected or their printed books looking nothing like what they saw on screen.

The Thumbnail Problem With Canva Covers

One more thing worth mentioning specifically about covers: Canva templates are designed to look good on screen at full size. They're not always optimized for what actually matters in self-publishing — which is how the cover looks at thumbnail size on Amazon.

A lot of Canva covers have intricate details, multiple fonts, or busy backgrounds that collapse into visual noise when shrunk to a product listing thumbnail. Readers can't read the title. The image becomes indistinct. The cover blends into the grid instead of standing out from it. A professional cover designer thinks about this from the start. Canva templates generally don't.

So Should You Use Canva for Your Book?

If you're making a short digital product, a simple eBook, or you need a quick mock-up to visualize your cover concept — yes, Canva is fine for that. It's free, it's fast, and it'll get the job done at that level.

But if you're publishing a full-length book — especially one going to print — Canva is going to create more problems than it solves. The formatting limitations, the export issues, and the lack of professional typographic controls mean the end result almost always looks like what it is: a book that was designed in a tool that wasn't built for books.

That gap between "looks okay on screen" and "looks professional in print" is exactly what separates a self-published book that readers trust from one they quietly put back on the shelf.

What the Alternative Looks Like

At Literary Lab, we use Adobe InDesign — the industry standard for book design and layout. The difference in output quality is significant. Proper typesetting, print-ready files, and a design built specifically for your book rather than adapted from a generic template.

If you've been trying to make Canva work for your book and running into walls, that's the most common sign it's time to bring in a professional. We handle the whole thing — interior formatting and cover design — and deliver files that are ready to upload and publish.

Reach out to us here and we'll talk through what your book needs.

Canva is a great tool. It's just not the right tool for every job — and book publishing is one of the places where that distinction really matters.

Literary Lab is a book design and formatting studio for independent authors.

posts.title: Can ChatGPT Design a Book Cover? What AI Can and Can't Do for Your Book
posts.metaDescription: AI tools are everywhere right now, including in publishing. But can ChatGPT actually design your book cover? Here's a realistic look at where AI helps and where it falls short.
posts.contentHtml:
It's a fair question to ask in 2025. AI can write essays, generate images, summarize documents, and do a hundred other things that seemed impossible a few years ago. So why not book covers?

The short answer is: ChatGPT itself can't design your book cover. But the longer answer is more interesting — because AI image tools absolutely can generate visuals, and some authors are using them. The real question is whether those visuals are good enough to publish with, and the answer to that is a lot more complicated.

What ChatGPT Actually Does

Let's clear up the confusion first. ChatGPT is a language model. It processes and generates text. It can help you brainstorm cover concepts, write your back cover blurb, describe a visual direction for your designer to follow, or give you feedback on cover ideas. That's genuinely useful stuff.

But it cannot generate images on its own. When people say "ChatGPT designed my cover," what they usually mean is they used an AI image generator — Midjourney, DALL·E, Adobe Firefly, Stable Diffusion — and ChatGPT may have helped them write the prompt. Those are different tools doing different things, and it's worth understanding the distinction before you go down that road.

AI Image Generators and Book Covers — The Real Situation

AI image generators have gotten remarkably good at producing visually striking images. If you feed Midjourney a detailed prompt, it can output something that looks genuinely beautiful. For certain genres — fantasy, science fiction, some romance — the results can be impressive enough that you could see them working as a cover.

But "impressive-looking image" and "effective book cover" are not the same thing. A book cover isn't just an image. It's a typographic design built around an image, sized and formatted for specific print and digital dimensions, with a title treatment that works at thumbnail scale, a spine, a back cover with a barcode, and files that meet the technical requirements of your printer or distributor.

AI tools generate images. They don't do any of the rest of that. You still need a designer to take whatever the AI produces and turn it into an actual publishable cover file. So the question isn't really whether AI can replace a designer — it's whether using AI-generated art as source material saves you enough time and money to be worth the trade-offs.

The Trade-Offs Nobody Talks About

The first issue is quality control. AI-generated images are inconsistent. You might generate fifty variations before finding one that works, and even then it might have subtle problems — distorted text embedded in the image, strange anatomy, backgrounds that don't tile correctly, details that fall apart when the image is enlarged for print.

The second issue is rights. The legal landscape around AI-generated images and copyright is still genuinely unsettled. Some platforms and distributors have started requiring disclosure of AI-generated content. Others have restrictions on it entirely. Publishing a book with an AI cover could create complications down the road that aren't worth the cost savings upfront.

The third issue — and this one matters most — is genre fit. Book cover design is not just about aesthetics. It's about communicating the right signals to the right readers. A designer who knows your genre understands what works and why. An AI tool generating images from a prompt has no concept of what a thriller reader expects to see versus what a literary fiction reader expects. The result can look polished and still miss completely in terms of market positioning.

Where AI Actually Helps in the Publishing Process

To be fair, AI has found genuinely useful places in book production — just not where most people expect. AI tools can help you refine your back cover copy, write author bios, generate multiple variations of a blurb to test, or research comparable titles in your genre. Some design tools now use AI to suggest layout adjustments or color palettes, which can speed up a professional designer's workflow.

Used as an assistant rather than a replacement, AI is a real asset. The mistake is expecting it to replace the judgment and craft of someone who has spent years learning what makes a book design work.

What Actually Gets Your Book Noticed

A great book cover comes from someone who understands your genre, knows what readers in that space respond to, can handle the typography with real skill, and delivers files that are technically ready for publishing. That's not something you can fully automate yet — and the books that perform well on Amazon reflect that.

At Literary Lab, we design book covers the way they should be designed — by hand, with real craft, built around your specific book and the readers you're trying to reach. We're not opposed to using modern tools where they help, but the design judgment behind every cover we produce is human.

If you're weighing your options and want to talk through what your cover needs, get in touch with us here.

AI is changing a lot of things in publishing. But for now, the covers that actually sell books are still being made by designers who know what they're doing.

Literary Lab is a book design and formatting studio for independent authors.

posts.title: How Much Does It Cost to Self-Publish a Book? A Realistic Breakdown
posts.metaDescription: Self-publishing costs vary wildly depending on what you cut corners on. Here's an honest breakdown of what you'll actually spend to publish a book properly in 2025.
posts.contentHtml:
One of the biggest myths about self-publishing is that it's free. Technically, uploading a book to Amazon KDP costs nothing. But publishing a book and publishing a good book are two very different things — and the gap between them almost always comes down to what you invested before you hit upload.

If you're planning to self-publish seriously, here's a realistic picture of what it actually costs.

Editing — The One You Really Shouldn't Skip

Before we even get to design, editing deserves a mention because it's the expense most authors try to avoid and later regret the most. A book that hasn't been professionally edited will get found out in reviews, and once those reviews are public, they're very hard to recover from.

Developmental editing — the kind that looks at structure, pacing, and whether your book is actually working as a whole — can run anywhere from $1,000 to $5,000 or more depending on the length of your manuscript and the editor you hire. Copyediting, which catches grammar, consistency, and style issues, is typically less expensive, ranging from a few hundred dollars to a couple thousand. Proofreading is the final pass before publication and usually the most affordable of the three.

A lot of self-published authors skip developmental editing and just get a proofread. That's a personal call — but it's worth going in with clear eyes about what you're getting.

Book Cover Design — Where First Impressions Are Made

Your cover is the first thing a reader sees and the most powerful marketing tool your book has. This is not the place to cut corners, and yet it's where many self-published authors try to save money the most.

A professional book cover design typically costs anywhere from $200 to $800 for a solid indie designer, and can go significantly higher for more established designers or complex illustrated covers. What you're paying for isn't just the visual — it's the genre knowledge, the typographic skill, the technical file preparation for print, and the understanding of what actually works on Amazon.

Pre-made covers are a cheaper option — you can find them in the $50 to $150 range — and they're a legitimate choice if you're working with a tight budget and find one that fits your book well. Just know that pre-made covers are sold to multiple authors, so there's no exclusivity.

Interior Formatting — The Cost of Looking Professional Inside

Interior book formatting is the part readers interact with on every single page, and it's chronically undervalued by authors who've never held a poorly formatted book next to a well-formatted one.

Professional interior formatting typically runs from $150 to $500 depending on the length and complexity of your manuscript — a straightforward novel is simpler than a non-fiction book with images, charts, and multiple heading levels. What you're getting is a book that reads cleanly, meets print specifications, and looks like it came from a traditional publishing house rather than something assembled in a word processor.

At Literary Lab, we handle both cover design and interior formatting, so you get everything you need to publish in one place without chasing multiple vendors and trying to get their files to work together.

ISBNs — A Small but Real Cost

If you're publishing through Amazon KDP only, you can use a free ISBN from KDP. The catch is that it ties you to Amazon — the publisher of record on that ISBN is KDP, not you.

If you want to publish across multiple platforms — IngramSpark, Barnes and Noble Press, Apple Books — or if you want to own your ISBNs as an independent publisher, you'll need to purchase your own through Bowker in the US. A single ISBN is $125. A block of ten is $295, which makes more sense if you're planning to publish multiple books or multiple editions.

This is a real cost that often catches first-time self-publishers off guard.

Marketing — The Ongoing Expense Nobody Budgets For

Publishing the book is just the beginning. Getting it in front of readers is its own investment entirely.

Amazon advertising, book promotion sites like BookBub, review campaigns, social media content, an author website — none of this is free, and most successful self-published authors spend more on marketing over the life of a book than they spent on production. This is a bigger conversation than one blog post can cover, but it's worth knowing upfront that publication day is not the finish line.

So What's the Total?

For a professionally produced self-published book — edited, designed, and formatted properly — a realistic budget is somewhere between $1,500 and $5,000 depending on how much editing work your manuscript needs and which services you choose for design and formatting.

You can publish for less. Plenty of authors do. But there's usually a visible trade-off in the final product, and in a market where readers are choosing between your self-published book and one from a traditional publisher, those trade-offs matter.

The investment in getting your book right isn't a luxury. It's what separates books that get taken seriously from books that get ignored.

Where Literary Lab Fits In

We work with self-publishing authors who want their book to compete — which means a cover that belongs in their genre and an interior that reads like a traditionally published book. Our services cover both, and we're upfront about pricing from the first conversation.

If you're putting together your publishing budget and want to know exactly what professional design and formatting will cost for your specific book, reach out to us here.

Self-publishing gives you control. Investing in quality gives you a chance. Both matter.

Literary Lab is a book design and formatting studio for independent authors.

posts.title: How Do I Create My Own Book? A Step-by-Step Guide for First-Time Authors
posts.metaDescription: Ready to turn your idea into a real book? Here's exactly how to go from blank page to published — written for first-time authors who want to get it right in 2026.
posts.contentHtml:
Everyone has a book somewhere inside them. Most people never get it out — not because they can't write, but because they don't know what actually happens between "I have an idea" and "here's my book on Amazon." The process feels murky, and murky is paralyzing.

So let's make it clear. Here's how creating a book actually works, from the first word to the moment it's live and in readers' hands.

Step One: Write the Thing

This sounds obvious but it needs to be said because a lot of people get stuck here longer than anywhere else. The writing phase is the one no tool, service, or shortcut can do for you. You have to sit down and produce the manuscript.

A few things that actually help: set a daily word count target rather than a total goal, because 1,000 words a day gets you a full-length book in three to four months and daily targets feel manageable in a way that "write 80,000 words" doesn't. Don't edit while you write — that's the fastest way to stay on chapter one forever. Get the full draft out first, messy as it is, and clean it up after.

Whether you're writing fiction, a memoir, a business book, or a guide of some kind, the manuscript comes first. Everything else builds on top of it.

Step Two: Edit Before You Do Anything Else

The single most common mistake first-time authors make is rushing from draft to publishing without putting their manuscript through proper editing. You cannot edit your own work effectively — you're too close to it. You'll read what you meant to write rather than what's actually on the page.

At minimum, get a solid copy edit. A good copyeditor catches grammar issues, consistency problems, awkward sentences, and the hundred small things that slip through even careful self-review. If you can invest in developmental editing — which looks at the bigger picture of structure, pacing, and whether the book is doing what you want it to do — even better.

Your book will be judged on its writing quality before anything else. The editing is what makes the writing as good as it can be.

Step Three: Get Your Book Professionally Designed

Once your manuscript is final — fully edited, not "mostly done" — it's time for design. This covers two things: the interior layout and the cover.

The interior is everything inside the book. Fonts, spacing, margins, chapter openers, page numbers, front matter and back matter. Done well it's invisible — readers just find the book comfortable to read. Done poorly it signals immediately that something is off, even to readers who can't explain why.

The cover is your primary marketing asset. In 2026, most book discovery happens online, which means your cover needs to work as a thumbnail on Amazon, as an image on Instagram, and as a full-size design on your website. A cover that looks decent on a desktop monitor but blurs into nothing at thumbnail size is not doing its job.

This is the part of the process where bringing in a professional pays off most visibly. At Literary Lab, we handle both — interior formatting and cover design — for authors who want their book to look like it belongs next to traditionally published titles.

Step Four: Get Your ISBN and Choose Your Distribution

Before you upload anything, you need to decide how your book will be distributed and whether you want your own ISBN.

Amazon KDP is where most self-published authors start, and for good reason — it's the largest book marketplace in the world and the setup is straightforward. But publishing exclusively on KDP means you're not in bookstores, not on Apple Books, not available through library systems. If wider distribution matters to you, IngramSpark gets your book into the broader retail and library network.

On the ISBN question: KDP offers a free ISBN, but it lists KDP as the publisher of record. If you want to publish as your own imprint — your own small press — you'll need to purchase your ISBNs through Bowker. It's an extra cost but it gives you control over your publishing identity long-term.

Step Five: Upload, Proof, and Publish

With your files ready — a print-ready PDF for the interior and a full cover spread with proper bleed and trim settings — you upload to your chosen platform and go through their review process.

Order a physical proof copy before you approve the book for sale. This is non-negotiable for print editions. What looks right on screen doesn't always translate to print, and catching a formatting issue on a proof copy costs you a few days and the price of a book. Catching it after you've sold a hundred copies costs you reputation.

Once the proof looks right, you approve it and your book goes live. That's the moment it becomes real.

The Part That Comes After

Publishing is not the end — it's the beginning of the next challenge, which is getting people to find and read your book. Marketing, reviews, social media presence, Amazon advertising — that's a whole conversation on its own. But none of it works well if the book itself isn't well-made.

The foundation of everything is a book that looks professional, reads well, and earns the trust of readers who've never heard of you before. That's what good writing, good editing, and good design produce together.

If you're at the design stage and you want it done properly, Literary Lab is here. We'll take the formatting and cover off your plate so you can focus on everything else.

Creating a book is one of the most satisfying things a person can do. The process is more manageable than it looks — you just have to know what order to do things in.

Literary Lab is a book design and formatting studio for independent authors.

posts.title: How Much Does an Author Make on a $20 Book? The Real Numbers
posts.metaDescription: Wondering what you actually earn when someone buys your book? Here's an honest breakdown of author royalties in 2026 — and why your book's design affects how much you make.
posts.contentHtml:
This is one of those questions where the answer sounds simple until you actually dig into it. How much does an author make on a $20 book? It depends on several things — where the book is sold, how it's published, whether it's print or digital, and whether you went through a traditional publisher or did it yourself.

Let's go through the real numbers.

Traditional Publishing Royalties — Lower Than Most People Think

If you signed with a traditional publisher and your book retails at $20, your cut as the author is likely somewhere between 10% and 15% of the cover price — and that's before your agent takes their 15% commission.

So on a $20 book, you might be looking at $2.00 to $3.00 per copy before the agent fee. After the agent, it's closer to $1.70 to $2.55 per copy. And if the publisher gave you an advance — which is common — you don't see a single royalty payment until your book earns back the entire advance first. That process, called "earning out," takes years for most authors and never happens at all for a significant portion of traditionally published books.

The trade-off for those lower royalties is that the publisher handles editing, design, distribution, and a portion of marketing. You give up earnings in exchange for infrastructure and credibility.

Self-Publishing Royalties on Amazon KDP — A Very Different Picture

This is where self-publishing gets genuinely interesting. On Amazon KDP, if you price your eBook between $2.99 and $9.99, you earn a 70% royalty. Below $2.99 or above $9.99, it drops to 35%.

For a $20 print book through KDP, the royalty structure is different because there are printing costs involved. Amazon calculates your royalty as 60% of the list price minus the printing cost. For a standard 300-page paperback at $20, printing costs run roughly $4.50 to $6.00 depending on page count and paper type. So you're looking at something like $20 × 60% = $12.00, minus say $5.00 in printing costs, leaving you with around $7.00 per copy sold.

That's a dramatically better per-copy earning than traditional publishing. The catch is that you're responsible for everything the publisher used to handle — including design, editing, and all your own marketing.

IngramSpark Changes the Math Slightly

If you publish through IngramSpark instead of or alongside KDP, the royalty structure is similar but not identical. IngramSpark charges higher printing costs in some cases but gets your book into wider distribution — physical bookstores, libraries, international retailers. The royalty per copy might be a little lower than KDP direct, but you're reaching readers you'd never reach on Amazon alone.

For a $20 print book through Ingram's extended distribution network, a realistic royalty after printing and distribution fees is somewhere between $4.00 and $7.00 depending on your specific book specs.

Why Your Book's Design Directly Affects Your Earnings

Here's the part most financial breakdowns of author royalties leave out entirely: your book's design affects how many copies you sell, which affects your total income more than any royalty percentage calculation.

A professionally designed cover gets more clicks on Amazon. More clicks mean more sales. A book that looks self-made — inconsistent formatting, a cover that doesn't fit the genre, typography that feels off — loses potential readers at the moment of discovery, before they've even read a word. That's lost income that never shows up in any royalty statement because the sale never happened.

Think about it this way. If a well-designed book sells 500 copies and a poorly designed book sells 100 copies, the better-designed book earns five times more — even if the royalty rate is identical. The investment in professional design doesn't just make your book look better. It directly impacts how much money you take home.

The Volume Reality

One more thing worth being honest about: most books, self-published or traditionally published, don't sell in huge numbers. The median self-published book sells fewer than 100 copies. That's not meant to be discouraging — it's meant to reframe how you think about the investment in your book.

The authors who build real income from self-publishing typically do it through volume — multiple books, a series, a backlist that compounds over time — rather than betting everything on one title. Each new book you publish sends readers back to your previous ones, and a consistent, professional look across your catalog builds reader trust in a way that a single book rarely can.

What This Means If You're Publishing in 2026

The economics of self-publishing are genuinely favorable right now compared to what was possible even five years ago. You can earn more per copy than a traditionally published author, keep creative control, and publish on your own timeline. But you have to put the work in on the front end — which means editing, design, and cover quality are not optional extras. They're the foundation your earnings are built on.

At Literary Lab, we work with self-publishing authors who understand this. A professionally formatted interior and a cover that competes in your genre isn't a luxury spend — it's part of the business case for publishing in the first place.

If you want to talk through what professional design would cost for your book, reach out to us here.

The royalty math can work very well in your favor as a self-published author. But only if people actually buy the book — and that starts with making it look worth buying.

Literary Lab is a book design and formatting studio for independent authors.

posts.title: The 5 Basic Principles of Design — And How They Apply to Your Book
posts.metaDescription: The 5 basic principles of design aren't just for graphic designers. They directly shape how your book looks, feels, and performs with readers. Here's what they mean for authors in 2026.
posts.contentHtml:
Design principles get taught in art schools and applied by professionals every day — but most authors never think about them in relation to their own books. That's a mistake, because every design decision in your book, whether it was made intentionally or by default, is either working with these principles or against them.

Understanding them doesn't mean you need to become a designer. It means you'll understand why a professional book looks the way it does, and why the difference between a well-designed book and a poorly designed one is never random.

1. Contrast

Contrast is what makes elements on a page distinguishable from each other. It's the difference between your chapter title and your body text. It's the reason a black title on a white cover is readable and a light grey title on a white cover is not. It's how hierarchy gets communicated visually — the reader's eye needs to be able to tell, at a glance, what's most important on any given page.

In book design, contrast shows up everywhere. A chapter title needs to contrast strongly with the body text beneath it — in size, weight, or both. A cover needs enough contrast between the title text and the background image that the title reads clearly even as a small thumbnail. Pull quotes in a non-fiction book need visual contrast to separate them from the main text without interrupting the reading flow.

When contrast is weak, everything looks flat and samey. The reader's eye has no natural place to land. It's one of the most common problems in amateur book design — and one of the easiest to fix once you know to look for it.

2. Alignment

Nothing says "made by someone who doesn't know what they're doing" quite as quickly as inconsistent alignment. Alignment is the invisible grid that holds a page together. When elements are properly aligned — text blocks, images, page numbers, headings — the page feels ordered and intentional. When they're not, something feels slightly off even if the reader can't articulate why.

In a book interior, alignment means your text starts at the same horizontal position on every page, your page numbers sit in a consistent location, your chapter openers are formatted identically throughout the entire manuscript. In cover design, it means your title, subtitle, and author name relate to each other spatially in a way that feels deliberate rather than dropped onto the page.

Professional book designers work with grids and guides that are invisible in the final product but present in every decision. That underlying structure is what makes the difference between a page that feels clean and a page that feels like it was assembled without a plan.

3. Repetition

Repetition is what creates consistency, and consistency is what creates a professional-feeling book. Every time a design element appears more than once in your book — a chapter heading style, a section break ornament, a header format, a color used on the cover — it should look exactly the same every time it appears.

This sounds basic, but it's where a lot of self-formatted books quietly fall apart. The chapter title is one size in chapter one and slightly different in chapter seven. The section break is a line of asterisks in some places and a decorative symbol in others. The margins drift slightly between sections because different parts of the manuscript were pasted in from different documents.

Repetition done well creates a rhythm that readers feel without noticing. It signals that someone made considered choices and followed through on them from the first page to the last. That sense of coherence is a huge part of what makes a book feel trustworthy — and trustworthy books get finished and recommended.

4. Proximity

Proximity is about grouping. Elements that belong together should be physically close to each other on the page. Elements that are separate ideas should have space between them. This sounds obvious, but it has real implications for how readers process information and how naturally they move through your book.

In a non-fiction book, proximity determines how subheadings relate to the text beneath them versus the text above them — a subheading should feel closer to what follows it than to what precedes it. In a cover design, the author name, title, and subtitle should be grouped in a way that communicates their relationship clearly. A cover where the author name floats halfway between the title and the bottom of the page with no clear visual logic is a proximity problem.

When proximity is handled well, the page organizes itself. The reader doesn't have to work to understand where to look or how the pieces relate. When it's handled badly, the page feels confused — even if everything on it is technically correct.

5. White Space

White space might be the most misunderstood principle in design, especially for authors who are thinking about page count and printing costs. Empty space feels wasteful. Why leave a big margin when you could fit more text?

Because the text is why the book exists — and white space is what makes the text readable. Generous margins give the eye a frame. Spacing between paragraphs creates natural breathing room that makes long reading sessions less tiring. Spacing around a chapter title gives it weight and importance. A cover with some clear, open space feels confident rather than cluttered.

The books that feel expensive and carefully made — the ones you pick up and immediately want to read — almost always have more white space than you'd expect. It's a counterintuitive truth about design: restraint reads as quality. Cramming every inch of a page signals that nobody thought carefully about the reader's experience.

Why This Matters for Your Book Specifically

Every one of these principles is either working for your book or working against it. If your book was formatted in Word without much thought given to design, the chances are high that contrast, alignment, repetition, proximity, and white space are all being handled inconsistently — and readers are feeling that, even if they can't name it.

At Literary Lab, these principles aren't a checklist. They're the foundation of every formatting and cover design decision we make. A book we design is built on a proper grid, with intentional typography, consistent treatment from page one to the end, and enough breathing room that it's a pleasure to read.

If you want your book designed with all of this applied properly, get in touch with us here.

Good design isn't magic. It's the consistent, disciplined application of principles that have been understood for a very long time. The best-looking books just make that look effortless.

Literary Lab is a book design and formatting studio for independent authors.
